title: Program Structure & Entry Points
subtitle: From public static void main to a clean script

sections.java: // HelloWorld.java public class HelloWorld { public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("Hello, World!"); } }
sections.python: # hello.py print("Hello, World!") # That's it. No class wrapper needed. # No main method. No semicolons. # No compilation step.

sections.title: Java Instinct to Override
sections.content: Your muscle memory will want to write public class wrappers. Resist it — Python files ARE modules. A file utils.py is directly importable: import utils.

sections.java: // Java: a function MUST live in a class. // Utility classes exist only to hold statics. public final class TextUtils { private TextUtils() {} // no instances public static String slugify(String s) { return s.toLowerCase().replace(' ', '-'); } } // Call: TextUtils.slugify("Hello World")
sections.python: # text_utils.py — the module IS the namespace def slugify(s: str) -> str: return s.lower().replace(" ", "-") def word_count(s: str) -> int: return len(s.split()) # Call: text_utils.slugify("Hello World") # No class. No static. No private constructor.

sections.title: You Can Build a Whole App Without a Single Class
sections.content: Java forces every function into a class — which is why the JDK is full of utility classes (Collections, Files, Math) that are just bags of statics. Python's module is already the namespace, so top-level functions are the norm, not a code smell. Plenty of production Python apps — CLIs, data pipelines, web handlers — are organized purely as modules and functions. Reach for a class only when you have state and behavior to bundle together; otherwise a function is the right unit. This is the heart of Python's function-first style.

sections.java: // Structured Java app package com.example.app; import java.util.List; public class App { private String name; public App(String name) { this.name = name; } public static void main(String[] args) { App app = new App("Demo"); app.run(); } }
sections.python: # app.py from typing import Optional class App: def __init__(self, name: str): self.name = name def run(self): print(f"Running {self.name}") if __name__ == "__main__": app = App("Demo") app.run()

sections.title: The __name__ Guard
sections.content: if __name__ == "__main__" is Python's equivalent of main(). It runs only when the file is executed directly, not when imported as a module. This is how Python separates "library code" from "script entry point".

sections.question: In Python, what replaces Java's curly braces {} for code blocks?
sections.explanation: Python uses consistent indentation (typically 4 spaces) to define code blocks. The colon : marks the start, then everything indented underneath belongs to that block.
